learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a critical and in-depth understanding of key topics in an area of psychology.
Demonstrate an ability to communicate orally and in writing about a topic in an area of psychology.
Demonstrate an understanding of how different types of research methods can contribute to our understanding of an area of psychology.
Effectively engage with others to discuss and evaluate empirical research.

assessments.due_date:
4/08/2026 - 27/10/2026

Students need to attend a minimum of 6 classes to pass the course.
assessments.task_description:
Full participation marks can be gained by actively participating in class discussions. The total participation mark for the course is computed on the basis of the 7 highest participation marks. In the first session, everyone who attends will get full participation marks. From the second session onwards, students will get half participation marks for attending, with the remaining half participation mark depending on their active contribution to the discussion. Presenters will always get full participation marks for the day of their presentation.

Alternative assessments can be discussed: Students who are afraid of speaking out in front of others can write a second essay (on one of the essay questions in the list; due on 30/09) to re-gain partial or full participation marks (depending on the quality of the essay). Students who would like to make use of the alternative assessment should contact the course organiser beforehand.

Students need to attend a minimum of 6 classes to pass the course.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: The assessment will be completed under supervised examination conditions. Students will be required to submit their responses at the end of the allocated time for the exam, so that we do not expect any late submissions.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.due_date:
End of Semester Exam Period

7/11/2026 - 21/11/2026

The purpose of this exam is to assess each student's ability to understand and critically evaluate the scientific literature, apply sound judgement, and develop and communicate their own ideas. Accordingly, the exam must be completed independently and reflect each student's own work. Collaboration with other students is not permitted.

This is an open book exam, which means that students are allowed to bring printed copies of the paper and printed course materials, including their notes and other journal articles or books. The use of generative artificial intelligence tools (e.g., ChatGPT and similar AI systems) is not permitted for any aspect of this exam.

The exam will be administered via a secure browser that restricts access to the exam website, such as Inspera's secure exam browser. No (other) electronic devices will be allowed for the exam.
assessments.task_description:
Students are required to complete a final examination consisting of short-answer questions based on a research paper that will be provided two weeks prior to the examination. The examination will assess students' understanding of the key concepts, findings, and theoretical issues addressed in the paper, as well as their knowledge of the research question and the methods used to investigate it.

Students will also be required to critically evaluate the strengths and limitations of the study and may be asked to outline an alternative experiment that addresses the research question in a more rigorous manner while overcoming the limitations of previous work.
assessments.submission_guidelines: The test will be administered through Inspera using a secure examination browser.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.late_submission: The assessment will be completed under supervised examination conditions. Students will be required to submit their responses at the end of the allocated time for the exam, so that we do not expect any late submissions.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

assessments.assessment_title: Oral presentation on a selected topic
assessments.task_description:
Each student is required to deliver an oral presentation on one of the papers discussed in the seminar, or on a comparable paper addressing the same topic, subject to approval by the course coordinator.

Presentations should be approximately 15 minutes and should provide a clear and critical overview of the study. Presenters are expected to address the theoretical background and motivation for the research, the research questions and hypotheses, the methodology, key findings, and the authors' conclusions. In addition, presentations should discuss the implications of the findings for relevant theories and for the interpretation of previous research in the area.

The final part of the presentation should provide a critical evaluation of the paper and suggest alternative approaches that can overcome the limitations of the current study and provide a better test of the research question.

Students who are unable to attend their scheduled presentation should notify the course coordinator as soon as possible. While alternative arrangements may be considered in exceptional circumstances, successful completion of the presentation component cannot be guaranteed if a scheduled presentation is missed.

The purpose of the presentation is to assess each student's ability to understand and critically evaluate the scientific literature, identify strengths and limitations of existing research, and develop their own interpretations and ideas. Students may use all course materials as well as published academic sources, including peer-reviewed journal articles in preparing their presetnations. However, the use of generative artificial intelligence tools (e.g., ChatGPT and similar AI systems) is not permitted, as this undermines the learning objectives and compromises the integrity of the assessment.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students are responsible for bringing an electronic copy of their presentation slides on the day of their presentation (e.g., on a USB drive).
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

learning_activities.topic:
Overview and Introduction to the Course

Introduction into Attention Research. Overview of Learning Resources and Assessments. Distribution of Student Presentations. To prepare, students can check Wikipedia. Wikipedia on Attention: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attention, and Wikipedia on Visual Search: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visual_search

Students should read the paper below before the seminar (Wolfe, 1994). The other papers are optional readings.

Wolfe, J. M. (1994). Guided Search 2.0: A revised model of visual search. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 1, 202-238.


Optional Readings:

Treisman & Sato (1990): Conjunction Search Revisited. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 16, 459-478.

Duncan & Humphreys (1989): Visual search and stimulus similarity. Psychological Review, 96, 433-458.

Posner, M. (1980). Orienting of Attention. Quarterly Journal of Experimental Psychology, 32, 3-25. 

Ruz, M. & Lupianez, J. (2002): A review of attentional capture: On its automaticity and sensitivity to endogenous control. Psicologica, 23, 283-309.

learning_activities.topic:
Bottom-Up vs Top-Down controlled factors in attention (Student Presentations and Discussion)

All students should read the following two papers:

Theeuwes, J. (1992). Perceptual selectivity for color and form. Perception & Psychophysics, 51, 599-606.

Ludwig, C. J. H., & Gilchrist, I. D. (2002). Stimulus-driven and goal-driven control over visual selection. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 28, 902-912.


Optional Reading:

Theeuwes, J. (2010). Top-down and bottom-up control of visual selection. Acta Psychologica, 135, 77-99.

Ludwig, C.J.H. & Gilchrist, I.D. (2003). Goal-driven modulation of oculomotor capture. Perception & Psychophysics, 65, 1243-1251.

learning_activities.topic:
No Seminar: Cognition Conference

This is a public holiday (Royal Queensland Show Holiday) for the Lockyer Valley. At the same time, the course organiser will be attending the Cognition Conference, which is an all-day event held at Bond University.

learning_activities.topic:
Search modes determine Attentional Capture (Student Presentations and Discussion)

All students should read the following three papers:

Bacon, W.F. & Egeth, H.E. (1994). Overriding stimulus-driven attentional capture. Perception & Psychophysics, 55, 485-496.

Theeuwes, J. (2004). Top-down search strategies cannot override attentional capture. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 11, 65-70. (short); AND:

Leber, A. & Egeth, H.E. (2006): It’s under control: Top-down search strategies can override attentional capture. Psychonomic Bulletin & Review, 13, 132-138 (short).


Optional Reading:

Becker, S.I., Martin, A. & Hamblin-Frohman, Z. (2019). Target templates in singleton search vs. feature-based search modes. Visual Cognition, 27, 502-517.

Luck, S. J., Gaspelin, N., Folk, C. L., Remington, R. W., & Theeuwes, J. (2021). Progress toward resolving the attentional capture debate. Visual Cognition, 29, 1 21.

learning_activities.topic:
Surprise Capture (Student Presentations and Discussion)

All students should read the following two papers:

Gibson, B.S., & Jiang, Y. (1998). Surprise! An unexpected color singleton does not capture attention in visual search. Psychological Science, 9, 176-182.

Horstmann, G. (2002). Evidence for attentional capture by a surprising color singleton in visual search. Psychological Science, 13, 499-505.


Optional Readings:

Ernst, D., Becker, S.I., & Horstmann, G. (2020). Novelty competes with saliency for attention. Vision Research, 168, 42-52.

Horstmann, G., Becker, S.I. & Ernst, D. (2016): Perceptual salience captures the eyes on a surprise trial. Attention, Perception & Psychophysics, 78, 1889-1900.

Retell, J.D., Venini, D., & Becker, S.I. (2015). Oculomotor capture by new and unannounced color singletons during visual search. Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 77, 1529-1543.

Retell, J.D., Becker, S.I., & Remington, R.W. (2016). Previously seen and expected stimuli elicit surprise in the context of visual search. Attention, Perception, & Psychophysics, 78, 774-788. doi: 10.3758/s13414-015-1052-9.

learning_activities.topic:
Inattentional Blindness (Student Presentations and Discussion)

All students should read the following two papers:

Simons, D.J., & Chabris, C.F. (1999): Gorillas in our midst: sustained inattentional blindness for dynamic events. Perception, 28, 1059-1074.

Most et al. (2001). How not to be seen: The contribution of similarity and selective ignoring to sustained inattentional blindness. Psychological Science, 12, 9-17.


Optional Reading:

Most, S. B., Scholl, B. J., Clifford, E. R., & Simons D. J. (2005). What you see is what you set: Sustained inattentional blindness and the capture of awareness. Psychological Review, 112, 217-242.

Koivisto, M., Hyona, J., & Revonsuo, A. (2004). The effects of eye movements, spatial attention, and stimulus features on inattentional blindness. Vision Research, 44, 3211-3221.

learning_activities.topic:
Effects of Reward and Punishment on Attentional Capture (Student Presentations and Discussion)

All students should read the following three papers:

Anderson, B.A., Laurent, P.A., & Yantis, S. (2011). Value-driven attentional capture. PNAS, 108, 10367-10371. AND:

Anderson, B.A., & Yantis, S. (2013). Persistence of value-driven attentional capture. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 39, 6-9. (à two very short Papers) - both papers for one presentation as they’re very short

Theeuwes, J. & Belopolsky, A.V. (2012). Reward grabs the eye: Oculomotor capture by rewarding stimuli. Vision Research, 74, 80-85.

Optional Reading:

Schmidt, Belopolsky & Theeuwes (2015). Attentional capture by signals of threat. Cognition & Emotion, 29, 687-694.

Pearson, Watson, Cheng & LePelly (2020): Overt attentional capture by reward-related stimuli overcomes inhibitory suppression. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 46, 489-501.

learning_activities.topic:
Effects of emotional expressions on attention (Student Presentations and Discussion)

All students should read the following two papers:

Devue, C. & Grimshaw, G.M. (2017). Faces are special, but facial expressions aren’t: insights from the oculomotor capture paradigm. Attention, Perception & Psychophysics, 79, 1438-1452.

Huang, S.L., Chang, Y.C. & Chen, Y.J. (2011). Task-irrelevant angry faces capture attention in visual search while modulated by attentional resources. Emotion, 11, 544-552.


Optional Readings:

Becker, S. I., Horstmann, G., & Remington, R. W. (2011). Perceptual grouping, not emotion, accounts for search asymmetries with schematic faces. Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 37, 1739-1757.

Becker, S.I., Dutt, N., Vromen, J.M.G. & Horstmann, G. (2017). The capture of attention and the gaze in search for emotional photographic faces. Visual Cognition, 25, 241-261.

Hunt, A.R., Cooper, R.M., Hungr, C. & Kingstone, A. (2007). The effect of emotional faces on eye movements and attention. Visual Cognition, 15(5), 513–531.

Coelho, C.M., Cloete, S. & Wallis, G. (2010). The face-in-the-crowd effect: when angry faces are just cross(es). Journal of Vision, 10(1), 1-14.

learning_activities.topic:
Threat Capture: Individual Difference vs. Conditioning Approaches (Student Presentations and Discussion)

All students should read the following two papers:

Lipp, O.V., & Waters, A.M. (2007). When danger lurks in the background: Attentional Capture by animal fear-relevant distractors is specific and selectively enhanced by animal fear. Emotion, 7, 192-200.

Schmidt, L.J., Belopolsky, A.V. & Theewues, J. (2015): Attentional capture by signals of threat. Cognition and Emotion, 29, 687-694.


Optional Readings:

Schmidt, L. J., Belopolsky, A. V., & Theeuwes, J. (2017). Oculomotor capture by stimuli that signal threat. Cognition and Emotion, 31(5), 1017-1024.

Devue, C., Belopolsky, A.V. & Theeuwes, J. (2011). The role of fear and expectancies in capture of covert attention by spiders. Emotion, 11, 768-775.

learning_activities.topic:
Relational vs. Optimal Tuning

All students should read the following two papers:

Hamblin-Frohman, Z. & Becker, S.I. (2021). The attentional template in high and low similarity search: Optimal tuning or tuning to relations? Cognition, 212.

Becker, S.I., Hamblin-Frohman, Z., Amarasekera, K.D.R. (2025). Visual search is relational without prior context learning. Cognition, 260.


Optional Reading:

Becker, S.I. (2013). Why you cannot map attention. A relational theory of attention and eye movements. Australian Psychologist, 48, 389-398. doi: 10.1111/ap.12028.

Becker, S.I., Harris, A.M., Venini, D., & Retell, J.D. (2014). Visual search for colour and shape: When is the gaze guided by feature relationships, when by feature values? Journal of Experimental Psychology: Human Perception and Performance, 40(1), 264-291. doi: 10.1037/a0033489.
